title: Buidisa East Africa Limited v Kenya Revenue Authority (Tribunal Appeal
text:
Buidisa East Africa Limited v Kenya Revenue Authority (Tribunal Appeal
E722 of 2025) [2026] KETAT 49 (KLR) (Civ) (26 March 2026) (Judgment)
Neutral citation: [2026] KETAT 49 (KLR)
REPUBLIC OF KENYA
IN THE TAX APPEAL TRIBUNAL
CIVIL
TRIBUNAL APPEAL E722 OF 2025
RO OLUOCH, CHAIR, C WESONGA & E KOMOLO, MEMBERS
MARCH 26, 2026
BETWEEN
BUIDISA EAST AFRICA LIMITED ...................................................... APPELLANT
AND
KENYA REVENUE AUTHORITY .................................................... RESPONDENT
JUDGMENT
1. The Appellant is a tax resident in Kenya registered under Personal Identication Number P0516317D.
2. The Respondent is the principal ocer appointed under section 13 of the Kenya Revenue Authority
Act. The Kenya Revenue Authority is an agency of the Government of Kenya mandated with the duty
of collecting and receiving all tax revenue, and the administration and enforcement of all tax laws set
out in Parts 1& 2 of the First Schedule to the Act, for purposes of assessing, collecting, and accounting
for all tax revenues in accordance with those laws.
3. The Appellant was issued a tax assessment totaling Kshs. 1,084,116 in relation to Value Added Tax
obligations for the 2024 nancial year.
4. The Appellant, aggrieved by the additional assessments, lodged an objection vide a letter dated 24th
April 2025.
5. On 9th June 2025, the Respondent issued an Objection Decision conrming the said assessments.
6. The Appellant, dissatised with the Objection Decision, led a Notice of Appeal on 15th July 2025
before this Tribunal.
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/49/eng@2026-03-26 1

The Appeal.
7. The Appellant, in its Memorandum of Appeal dated 22nd July 2025 and led on the same date, has set
out the following grounds of appeal:
8. That in February 2024, the Kenya Revenue Authority suspended the VAT obligation status of the
Appellant without prior notice or hearing. The said suspension remained in place until January 2025
leading to the following:
a. The Appellant was barred from accessing the iTax platform, making it impossible to le VAT
returns during this period.
b. The Appellant was unable to claim legitimate input VAT on purchases made in the course of
its business operations.
c. That the suspension had a direct impact on the ability of the Appellant to comply with VAT
ling obligations, as it continued with its business and in dealing with taxable supplies.
d. That the Authority’s assessment fails to account for this administrative action, which was
beyond the control of the company.
e. The Appellant submitted that this action by the Revenue Authority caused both procedural
and substantive prejudice and ought to have been considered in arriving at the tax assessment.
9. The Appellant stated that the current assessment fails to account for withholding tax payments
amounting to Kshs. 113,793 which were correctly deducted at source by the Appellant’s various clients
in accordance with the law and duly remitted to KRA within stipulated timelines.
10. That the omission of these credits from the assessment has overstated the Appellant’s tax liability and
resulted in an unjust assessment amount.
Appellant’s Case
11. The Appellant supported its Appeal with its Statement of Facts dated 15th July 2025 and led on 22nd
July 2025, where it argued as follows:
12. The Appellant stated that in February 2024 the KRA unilaterally suspended its VAT obligation status
without prior notice or the opportunity to be heard. That the suspension, which remained in eect
until January 2025 impacted the company adversely and aected its ability to comply with its VAT-
related obligations.
13. The Appellant submitted that it was barred from accessing the iTax platform, making it technically
impossible for it to le VAT returns during the aected period.
14. Furthermore, it was unable to claim legitimate input VAT on business-related purchases incurred in
the ordinary course of business operations.
15. The Appellant posited that the suspension impeded its ability to meet its various VAT compliance
requirements, despite the fact that the company continued to engage in taxable supplies and regular
business activities.
16. The Appellant contended that the Respondent’s current VAT assessment has failed to take into
account this administrative action, which was entirely beyond the Appellant’s control.
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/49/eng@2026-03-26 2

17. The Appellant averred that the assessment rendered by the Respondent does not accurately reect
withholding tax credits amounting to Kshs. 113,793 which were deducted at source by various clients
in accordance with the law and duly remitted to the Respondent within the stipulated timelines.
18. That its VAT non-compliance and the omission of withholding tax credits in the assessment stemmed
from factors either caused by administrative actions of the Respondent or from duly complied third-
party obligations and should not form the basis for punitive assessments or penalties.
19. The Appellant requested that the Tribunal consider the above facts and grant the appropriate relief in
accordance with the laws and principles of fairness.
Appellant’s Prayer
20. The Appellant’s prayer to the Tribunal was that the appeal be referred for Alternative Dispute
Resolution to facilitate a mutually agreeable and expeditious resolution.
Respondent's Case
21. The Respondent’s case is premised on:
a. Its Statement of Facts dated 19th August 2025 and led on the same date.
b. Its Written Submissions dated 18th February 2026 and led on the same date.
22. The Respondent stated that it conducted a compliance check on the Appellant to ascertain their tax
aairs, which led to the issuance of an additional assessment with respect to VAT for the month of
April 2024 amounting to Kshs. 1,112,382.17 which included the principal, tax, penalties, and interest.
23. That the Appellant, aggrieved by the said assessments, lodged an objection vide a letter dated 24th April
2025 which was received by the Respondent on 25th April 2025.
24. The Respondent averred that vide an email dated 13th May 2025, it requested the Appellant for
additional documents in support of their objection, with a further reminder sent on 20th May 2025,
but the Appellant neither responded nor provided the requested documents.
25. The Respondent stated that it issued its Objection Decision on 9th June 2025 fully rejecting the
objection and conrming the assessments. Dissatised with the objection decision, the Appellant led
this appeal dated 15th July 2025 which was served upon the Respondent on 23rd July 2025.
26. The Respondent observed that the Appellant conducted business with Meru County and failed to
remit the ensuing taxes, whereas Meru County withheld taxes amounting to Kshs. 113,793.
27. The Respondent stated that it requested the Appellant for the following documents relating to the
period under review:
a. Audited nancial statements
b. General ledgers, trial balances
c. Sales and purchase ledgers
d. Sales and purchase invoices
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/49/eng@2026-03-26 3

28. That the Appellant failed to avail the requested documents. The Respondent relied on Section 51(3)
of the Tax Procedures Act, which provides that a notice of objection shall be validly lodged if all relevant
documents relating to the objection have been submitted:
“A notice of objection shall be treated as validly lodged by a taxpayer under subsection (2) if:
(a) ------
(b) -----
(c) all the relevant documents relating to the objection have been submitted”
29. The Respondent stated that Section 24(2) of the TPA allows it to assess a taxpayer’s liability using any
information available to it and reiterated that it used its best judgment to raise the said assessment and
demanded taxes as empowered under Section 31 of the TPA.
30. The Respondent further stated that the Appellant did not submit the necessary records that would
have helped in the determination of its tax liability and therefore failed to discharge its burden of proof.
31. The Respondent contended that all its actions in arriving at the assessments were taken in accordance
with relevant laws and that the Appellant was given an opportunity to object to the said assessments
as stipulated by law.
32. The Respondent concluded by stating that the tax assessment decision was properly founded in law
and fact and that the Objection Decision rendered was fair, reasonable, and made in accordance with
statutory provisions.
33. The Respondent reiterated its averments in its Written Submissions dated 18th February, 2026. Further,
it supported its case with the following authorities:
a. Digital Box Limited v Commissioner of Domestic Taxes (TAT Appeal No. 115 of 2017)
b. Kenya Revenue Authority v Man Diesel & Turbo Se, Kenya (2021) eKLR.
Respondent’s Prayers
34. The Respondent prays that the Tribunal:
a. Dismisses the Appeal in its entirety.
b. Upholds the tax assessment as conrmed by the Objection Decision dated 9th June 2025; and
c. Orders the Appellant to pay the costs of the Appeal.
Issue For Determination
35. The Tribunal, having considered the pleadings and submissions of both parties, is of the view that the
issue that calls for its determination is whether the Respondent’s assessments were justied.
Analysis And Determination.
36. The Tribunal, having established the one issue for its determination, proceeds to analyze it as follows:
37. The dispute before the Tribunal primarily arose from the Objection Decision dated 9th June 2025
which conrmed the Respondent’s additional assessments upon the Appellant. This was after the
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/49/eng@2026-03-26 4

Appellant led its objection vide a letter dated 25th April 2025, which was subsequently rejected in full,
and the assessments were conrmed.
38. The Tribunal has reviewed the Objection Decision, which is on record, and notes that the Respondent
requested the following documents, which relate to the period under review, to validate the Appellant’s
objection to the additional assessments;
a. Audited nancial statements
b. General Ledgers
c. Sales & Purchase ledgers
d. Sales and Purchase Invoices
39. In its pleadings, the Respondent stated that the Appellant failed to provide necessary records to support
its objection in accordance with Section 59 (1) of the TPA and discharge its burden of proof as per
Section 56 of the TPA. Thus, the Appellant failed, according to the Respondent, to demonstrate that
the additional assessment was incorrect.
40. The Tribunal has perused the documents before it and is able to sight the audited nancial statements
for the year ended 2024 and the general ledger for the Appellant’s business. The remaining documents
have not been presented as evidence before this Tribunal.
41. Section 59 of the Tax Procedures Act (Cap 469B) provides as follows regarding the Appellant’s duty to
produce documents and records as may be sought by the Respondent: -
“(1) For the purposes of obtaining full information in respect of the tax liability of
any person or class of persons, or for any other purposes relating to a tax law,
the Commissioner or an authorized ocer may require any person, by notice
in writing, to –
(a) produce for examination, at such time and place as may be
specied in the notice, any documents (including in electronic
format) that are in the person’s custody or under the person’s
control relating to the tax liability of any person;
(b) furnish information relating to the tax liability of any person in
the manner and by the time specied in the notice; or
(c) attend, at the time and place specied in the notice, for the
purpose of giving evidence in respect of any matter or transaction
appearing to be relevant to the tax liability of any person.”
42. Section 56 (1) of the Tax Procedures Act (Cap 469B), which provides as follows regarding the
Appellant’s burden of proof: -
“In any proceedings under this Part, the burden shall be on the taxpayer to prove that a tax
decision is incorrect”
43. Furthermore, Section 30 of the Tax Appeals Tribunal Act (Cap 469A) provides as follows on the
Appellant’s burden of proof: -
“In a proceeding before the Tribunal, the Appellant has the burden of proving—
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/49/eng@2026-03-26 5

(a) where an appeal relates to an assessment, that the assessment is excessive; or
(b) in any other case, that the tax decision should not have been made or should
have been made dierently.”
44. In this instant Appeal, the Tribunal notes that the Appellant was not able to avail the full list of
documents requested by the Respondent. The Appellant failed to provide critical documents that
would have helped to interrogate the additional assessments as presented by the Respondent. In the
end, they made averments and did not provide the necessary evidence to support them.
45. The burden was thus on the Appellant to prove that the Respondent’s assessment was erroneous. The
easiest way to discharge this burden would have been by sharing the documents that the Respondent
asked for to prove that the assessments were inaccurate, excessive, or should have been made in a
dierent way.
46. The Appellant failed to provide relevant documents as requested by the Respondent, which would
have guided the Respondent in arriving at the conclusion that its tax assessment was wrong, erroneous,
erroneous,or excessive.
47. Without supporting documents, the Appellants' contentions remained mere averments. The Tribunal
nds guidance in the case of Alfred Kioko Muteti v. Timothy Miheso & Another (2015) eKLR where
it was held that;
“A party can only discharge its burden upon adducing evidence. ...........Merely making
pleadings is not enough."
48. The Honourable Tribunal has also previously reiterated the Appellant’s duty to discharge its burden of
proof in the rst instance. In Abyssinia Iron and Steel Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs and Border
Control (TAT No. 435 of 2022), the Tribunal held as follows: -
“The bottom line is that once the Appellant has provided evidence that the Respondent’s
assessment was wrong, then the Respondent must push back and show that its assessment
was not arbitrary, capricious, or imagined. The onus will then shift back to the Appellant
once the Respondent has discharged its burden on a balance of convenience to discharge
the prima facie case that has been presented by the Respondent.”
49. The fact that the burden of proof that the Respondent’s tax decision was wrong always rests on
the Appellant has been further armed by this Tribunal in Tumaini Distributors [K] Ltd -vs-
Commissioner of Domestic Taxes [2020] eKLR, where the court held that the taxpayer has the burden
to prove that the tax decision is wrong. Similarly, in the case of Otieno Odongo & Partners -vs-
Commissioner of Domestic Taxes TAT N O. 290 of 2019, again it was held that it is upon the Appellant
to prove that the Respondent’s decision is wrong.
50. Guided by the foregoing analysis, authorities, Section 56(1) of the TPA, and Section 30 of the TAT
Act, the Tribunal thus nds and determines that the Appellant failed in discharging its burden of proof
and that the Respondent’s Objection Decision was justied.
Disposition
51. The upshot of the foregoing is that the Appeal herein lacks merit, and the Tribunal accordingly
proceeds to make the following Orders:
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/49/eng@2026-03-26 6

a. The Appellant’s Appeal be and is hereby dismissed.
b. The Respondent’s Objection Decision dated 9th June 2025 be and is hereby upheld.
c. Each party to bear its own costs.
52. It is so ordered.
DATED AND DELIVERED AT NAIROBI THIS 26TH DAY OF MARCH 2026
......................................
DR. RODNEY ODHIAMBO OLUOCH
CHAIRPERSON
......................................
CAROLINE WESONGA DR. ERICK KOMOLO
MEMBER MEMBER
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/49/eng@2026-03-26 7
